title: Analytical Treatment of Noise-Suppressed Klein Tunneling in Graphene with Possible Implications for Quantum-Dot Qubits
authors: Kamal Azaidaoui, Ahmed Jellal, Hocine Bahlouli, A. Al Luhaibi, Michael Vogl
abstract: We study quantum tunneling through a potential barrier whose height fluctuates in time and is modeled by Gaussian white noise. We map the stochastic dynamics onto an equivalent time-independent Lindblad equation for the density matrix, allowing fully analytical solutions. For Schrödinger particles, noise introduces dissipation that suppresses Fabry-Pérot oscillations and yields an exponentially decaying transmission. Applying the same formalism to graphene, we demonstrate that noise induces a complex longitudinal wavevector within the barrier, leading to a strong suppression of transmission and Klein tunneling, even at normal incidence. Our approach promises improved control over Klein tunneling. These results demonstrate that noisy barriers can act as tunable dissipative elements, offering a pathway to enhanced control of electron transport in graphene-based devices. We also briefly discuss how our results could guide the design of graphene quantum dots for potential use in spin qubit devices.
summary: This paper explores how temporal fluctuations in a potential barrier can suppress the Klein tunneling effect in graphene. By using a Lindblad master equation approach, the authors show that noise introduces an imaginary component to the wavevector, causing exponential decay of transmission. This mechanism allows for the tuning of barrier opacity without changing the physical geometry. The findings suggest that controlled noise could improve electron confinement in graphene-based devices, such as quantum dots for spin qubits.
main_problem: Investigating how temporal noise (stochastic fluctuations in barrier height) can be used as a mechanism to suppress Klein tunneling in graphene.
main_result: Noise induces a complex longitudinal wavevector in graphene, leading to the exponential suppression of Klein tunneling even at normal incidence, effectively turning a noisy barrier into a tunable dissipative element.
method: The authors map the stochastic dynamics of a time-dependent potential onto a time-independent Lindblad master equation for the density matrix, which is then solved using a spatial matching technique in a nine-region construction.
model_or_system: The study models electrons in graphene (massless Dirac fermions) and non-relativistic Schrödinger particles traversing a 1D rectangular potential barrier whose height fluctuates according to Gaussian white noise.
key_observables: Transmission probability (T), reflection probability (R), and absorption probability (A), representing the loss of coherent flux.
important_parameters: Barrier height (U0), noise strength (V0), barrier width (L), incident energy (epsilon), and incident angle (phi).
main_assumptions: The noise is modeled specifically as Gaussian white noise (Markovian/memoryless), and the potential is assumed to be piecewise constant.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows a schematic of the noisy barrier; Fig 2 shows a realization of Gaussian white noise; Fig 3 shows the nine-region construction in the (x, x') plane; Fig 4 shows T and A for Schrödinger particles; Fig 6 shows angular dependence of T and A; Fig 7 shows energy dependence; Fig 8 compares clean vs. noisy double barriers for confinement.
paper_structure: The paper establishes a benchmark with Schrödinger particles, develops the Lindblad formalism for noise, applies it to graphene to demonstrate Klein tunneling suppression, and discusses applications to quantum dots.
why_interesting: The paper provides an analytical framework for treating stochastic driving as a control mechanism for transport, demonstrating how dissipation can be used to break symmetry-protected phenomena like Klein tunneling.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Quantum tunneling across a barrier of static height U0, whose amplitude fluctuates in time with noise strength V0. The temporal fluctuations introduced by noise are indicated by the red arrows. Moreover, the incident, reflected, and transmitted waves are schematically indicated.
FIG. 2. Sample realization of Gaussian white noise fluctua- tions, the noise has zero mean and shows δ temporal correla- tions, ⟨η(t)η(t′)⟩= δ(t −t′).

FIG. 3. Schematic of the nine regions in the (x, x′) plane used in the density-matrix scattering formalism. The vertical and horizontal lines at x = 0 and x = L, and at x′ = 0 and x′ = L, mark the interfaces of the noisy barrier. Each region (i, j) corresponds to x and x′ being in the left (x < 0), inside the barrier, (0 < x < L), or in the right lead (x > L).

FIG. 4. Transmission and absorption for a non-relativistic Schr¨odinger particle traversing a static and noisy barrier as a function of k0L. With k0 = √2mU0. The top row corresponds to electrons with energy ϵ = 1.1 U0 that is larger than the barrier height: (a) transmission T and (b) absorption A. The bottom row corresponds to the tunneling regime ϵ = 0.9 U0: (c) transmission T and (d) absorption A. Curves are shown for noise strength V0/U0 = 0 (red), 0.01 (blue), and 0.02 (green).

FIG. 7. Transmission (a) and absorption (b) probabilities for electrons in graphene tunneling through a noisy barrier as a function of incoming energy for an incident angle ϕ = 45◦, barrier width L = 110 nm, and static barrier height is U0 = 200 meV . The three curves correspond to noise strength V0 = 0 (red), 20 (blue), and 40 meV(green).

FIG. 6. Angular dependence of transmission and ab- sorption probabilities for tunneling through a noisy barrier in graphene. Panels (a,b) show the transmission T(ϕ) for U0 = 200 and 285 meV, and panels (c,d) show the correspond- ing absorption A(ϕ). The incoming energy is ϵ = 80 meV, the barrier width is L = 110 nm. Curves correspond to the noise strength V0 = 0, 20, and 40 meV.Parameters are chosen to facilitate comparison with standard Klein-tunneling setups in the literature, see Ref. [6].

FIG. 8. Illustration of Quantum confinement in two cases:(a) Clean double barrier where Klein tunneling dominates, and the particle cannot efficiently be confined. (b) A noisy double barrier, based on our calculations, could suppress Klein tun- neling and confine the particle.
